Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for finding a single table entry, by optimistically creating secondary indexes for primary keys and columns with uniqueness constraints. When the transaction commits, those indexes will already be created on disk.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for many small transactions. These transactions will all be optimistically written (assuming a ROLLBACK will not occur) to the database file, so the intermediate states do not have to be stored in temporary files.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. The schema is also shown in the image below:  Give the SQL query that returns the Name and total Revenue for Customers in the City of 'Nijmegen' in the Year 2021.
Answer: SELECT      (SELECT Name       FROM Customer_DIM       WHERE Customer_DIM.Customer_ID = Fact_Table.Customer_ID) AS Customer_Name,     SUM(Fact_Table.Revenue) AS Total_Revenue FROM      Fact_Table, Time_DIM WHERE      Fact_Table.Time_ID = Time_DIM.Time_ID     AND Fact_Table.Customer_ID IN (         SELECT Customer_ID          FROM Customer_DIM          WHERE City = 'Nijmegen'     )     AND Time_DIM.Year = 2021 GROUP BY      Fact_Table.Customer_ID;

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for finding a single table entry, by optimistically creating secondary indexes for primary keys and columns with uniqueness constraints. When the transaction commits, those indexes will already be created on disk.

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. The schema is also shown in the image below:  Give the SQL query that returns the Name and total Revenue for Customers in the City of 'Nijmegen' in the Year 2021.
Answer: SELECT      Customer_DIM.Name AS Customer_Name,      SUM(Fact_Table.Revenue) AS Total_Revenue FROM      Fact_Table, Customer_DIM, Time_DIM WHERE      Fact_Table.Customer_ID = Customer_DIM.Customer_ID     AND Fact_Table.Time_ID = Time_DIM.Time_ID     AND Customer_DIM.City = 'Nijmegen'     AND Time_DIM.Year = 2021 GROUP BY      Customer_DIM.Name;

Answer: SELECT      c.Name AS Customer_Name,      SUM(f.Revenue) AS Total_Revenue FROM      Fact_Table f JOIN      Customer_DIM c ON f.Customer_ID = c.Customer_ID JOIN      Time_DIM t ON f.Time_ID = t.Time_ID WHERE      c.City = 'Nijmegen'     AND t.Year = 2021 GROUP BY      c.Name;

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for loading large datasets, and will not be able to hold the transaction-local state in main memory. Instead of writing intermediate data to temporary files, it optimistically writes them to the database file (assuming a ROLLBACK is rare).

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for many small transactions. These transactions will all be optimistically written (assuming a ROLLBACK will not occur) to the database file, so the intermediate states do not have to be stored in temporary files.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for finding a single table entry, by optimistically creating secondary indexes for primary keys and columns with uniqueness constraints. When the transaction commits, those indexes will already be created on disk.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for many small transactions. These transactions will all be optimistically written (assuming a ROLLBACK will not occur) to the database file, so the intermediate states do not have to be stored in temporary files.

Answer: SELECT Customer_DIM.Name, SUM(Fact_Table.Revenue) FROM Fact_Table, Customer_DIM, Time_DIM WHERE Customer_DIM.Customer_ID=Fact_Table.Customer_ID AND Customer_DIM.City='Nijmegen' AND Time_DIM.Year=2021 AND Time_DIM.Time_ID=Fact_Table.Time_ID  GROUP BY Customer_DIM.Name;

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for finding a single table entry, by optimistically creating secondary indexes for primary keys and columns with uniqueness constraints. When the transaction commits, those indexes will already be created on disk.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for loading large datasets, and will not be able to hold the transaction-local state in main memory. Instead of writing intermediate data to temporary files, it optimistically writes them to the database file (assuming a ROLLBACK is rare).

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. Answer: SELECT c.Name, SUM(f.Revenue) AS Total FROM Fact_Table f JOIN Customer_DIM c ON f.Customer_ID = c.Customer_ID JOIN Time_DIM t ON f.Time_ID = t.Time_ID WHERE c.City = 'Nijmegen' AND t.Year = 2021 GROUP BY  c.Name;

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for finding a single table entry, by optimistically creating secondary indexes for primary keys and columns with uniqueness constraints. When the transaction commits, those indexes will already be created on disk.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. Answer: DuckDB prepares for a ROLLBACK or similar error during loading. In this case, discarding of the transaction-local state is free and optimistic.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB prepares for a ROLLBACK or similar error during loading. In this case, discarding of the transaction-local state is free and optimistic.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for loading large datasets, and will not be able to hold the transaction-local state in main memory. Instead of writing intermediate data to temporary files, it optimistically writes them to the database file (assuming a ROLLBACK is rare).

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. The schema is also shown in the image below:  Give the SQL query that returns the Name and total Revenue for Customers in the City of 'Nijmegen' in the Year 2021.
Answer: SELECT c.Name, SUM(f.Revenue) FROM Fact_Table f JOIN Customer_DIM c ON f.Customer_ID = c.Customer_ID JOIN Time_DIM t ON f.Time_ID = t.Time_ID WHERE c.City = 'Nijmegen' AND t.Year = 2021 GROUP BY c.Name;

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB prepares for a ROLLBACK or similar error during loading. In this case, discarding of the transaction-local state is free and optimistic.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for many small transactions. These transactions will all be optimistically written (assuming a ROLLBACK will not occur) to the database file, so the intermediate states do not have to be stored in temporary files.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Answer: DuckDB optimizes for finding a single table entry, by optimistically creating secondary indexes for primary keys and columns with uniqueness constraints. When the transaction commits, those indexes will already be created on disk.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Answer: DuckDB optimizes for loading large datasets, and will not be able to hold the transaction-local state in main memory. Instead of writing intermediate data to temporary files, it optimistically writes them to the database file (assuming a ROLLBACK is rare).

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. Answer: SELECT      c.Name AS Customer_Name,      SUM(f.Revenue) AS Total_Revenue FROM      Fact_Table f JOIN      Customer_DIM c ON f.Customer_ID = c.Customer_ID JOIN      Time_DIM t ON f.Time_ID = t.Time_ID WHERE      c.City = 'Nijmegen'      AND t.Year = 2021 GROUP BY      c.Name;

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for many small transactions. These transactions will all be optimistically written (assuming a ROLLBACK will not occur) to the database file, so the intermediate states do not have to be stored in temporary files.

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. The schema is also shown in the image below:  Give the SQL query that returns the Name and total Revenue for Customers in the City of 'Nijmegen' in the Year 2021.
Answer: SELECT Customer_DIM.Name, sum(Fact_Table.Revenue) FROM Fact_Table, Customer_DIM, Time_DIM WHERE Customer_DIM.Customer_ID = Fact_Table.Customer_ID AND Time_DIM.Time_ID = Fact_Table.Time_ID AND Customer_DIM.City = 'Nijmegen' AND Time_DIM.Year = 2021 GROUP BY Customer_DIM.Name;

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB prepares for a ROLLBACK or similar error during loading. In this case, discarding of the transaction-local state is free and optimistic.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for loading large datasets, and will not be able to hold the transaction-local state in main memory. Instead of writing intermediate data to temporary files, it optimistically writes them to the database file (assuming a ROLLBACK is rare).

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for finding a single table entry, by optimistically creating secondary indexes for primary keys and columns with uniqueness constraints. When the transaction commits, those indexes will already be created on disk.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for many small transactions. These transactions will all be optimistically written (assuming a ROLLBACK will not occur) to the database file, so the intermediate states do not have to be stored in temporary files.

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. The schema is also shown in the image below:  Give the SQL query that returns the Name and total Revenue for Customers in the City of 'Nijmegen' in the Year 2021.
Answer: SELECT Customer_DIM.Name, Fact_Table.Revenue FROM Fact_Table, Customer_DIM, Time_DIM WHERE Fact_Table.Customer_Id = Customer_DIM.Customer_id AND Time_DIM.Time_ID = Fact_TABLE.Time_ID AND Time_DIM.Year = 2021 AND Customer_DIM.City = 'Nijmegen';

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. The schema is also shown in the image below:  Give the SQL query that returns the Name and total Revenue for Customers in the City of 'Nijmegen' in the Year 2021.
Answer: SELECT c.Name, SUM(f.Revenue) AS Total_Revenue FROM Fact_Table f JOIN Customer_DIM c ON f.Customer_ID = c.Customer_ID JOIN Time_DIM t ON f.Time_ID = t.Time_ID WHERE c.City = 'Nijmegen'   AND t.Year = 2021 GROUP BY c.Name;

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB prepares for a ROLLBACK or similar error during loading. In this case, discarding of the transaction-local state is free and optimistic.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for finding a single table entry, by optimistically creating secondary indexes for primary keys and columns with uniqueness constraints. When the transaction commits, those indexes will already be created on disk.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for loading large datasets, and will not be able to hold the transaction-local state in main memory. Instead of writing intermediate data to temporary files, it optimistically writes them to the database file (assuming a ROLLBACK is rare).

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. The schema is also shown in the image below:  Give the SQL query that returns the Name and total Revenue for Customers in the City of 'Nijmegen' in the Year 2021.
Answer: SELECT Customer_DIM.Name, SUM(Fact_Table.Revenue)  FROM Fact_Table, Customer_DIM, Time_DIM WHERE Customer_DIM.City='Nijmegen' AND Time_DIM.Year=2021 AND Fact_Table.Time_ID=Time_DIM.Time_ID AND Fact_Table.Customer_ID=Customer_DIM.Customer_ID GROUP BY Customer_DIM.Name;

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. The schema is also shown in the image below:  Give the SQL query that returns the Name and total Revenue for Customers in the City of 'Nijmegen' in the Year 2021.
Answer: SELECT C.Name, SUM(F.Revenue) FROM Customer_DIM C, Fact_Table F, Time_DIM T WHERE F.Customer_ID = C.Customer_ID AND F.Time_ID = T.Time_ID AND C.City = 'Nijmegen' AND T.Year = 2021 GROUP BY C.Name;

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. The schema is also shown in the image below:  Give the SQL query that returns the Name and total Revenue for Customers in the City of 'Nijmegen' in the Year 2021.
Answer: SELECT      Customer_DIM.Name AS Customer_Name,      SUM(Fact_Table.Revenue) AS Total_Revenue FROM      Fact_Table JOIN      Customer_DIM ON Fact_Table.Customer_ID = Customer_DIM.Customer_ID JOIN      Time_DIM ON Fact_Table.Time_ID = Time_DIM.Time_ID WHERE      Customer_DIM.City = 'Nijmegen'     AND Time_DIM.Year = 2021 GROUP BY      Customer_DIM.Name;

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB prepares for a ROLLBACK or similar error during loading. In this case, discarding of the transaction-local state is free and optimistic.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for loading large datasets, and will not be able to hold the transaction-local state in main memory. Instead of writing intermediate data to temporary files, it optimistically writes them to the database file (assuming a ROLLBACK is rare).

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. The schema is also shown in the image below:  Give the SQL query that returns the Name and total Revenue for Customers in the City of 'Nijmegen' in the Year 2021.
Answer: SELECT      c.Name,     SUM(f.Revenue) AS Total_Revenue FROM      Fact_Table f JOIN      Customer_DIM c ON f.Customer_ID = c.Customer_ID JOIN      Time_DIM t ON f.Time_ID = t.Time_ID WHERE      c.City = 'Nijmegen'     AND t.Year = 2021 GROUP BY      c.Name;

Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?

When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for loading large datasets, and will not be able to hold the transaction-local state in main memory. Instead of writing intermediate data to temporary files, it optimistically writes them to the database file (assuming a ROLLBACK is rare).

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for finding a single table entry, by optimistically creating secondary indexes for primary keys and columns with uniqueness constraints. When the transaction commits, those indexes will already be created on disk.

Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for many small transactions. These transactions will all be optimistically written (assuming a ROLLBACK will not occur) to the database file, so the intermediate states do not have to be stored in temporary files.

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for finding a single table entry, by optimistically creating secondary indexes for primary keys and columns with uniqueness constraints. When the transaction commits, those indexes will already be created on disk.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB prepares for a ROLLBACK or similar error during loading. In this case, discarding of the transaction-local state is free and optimistic.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for loading large datasets, and will not be able to hold the transaction-local state in main memory. Instead of writing intermediate data to temporary files, it optimistically writes them to the database file (assuming a ROLLBACK is rare).

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB prepares for a ROLLBACK or similar error during loading. In this case, discarding of the transaction-local state is free and optimistic.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for finding a single table entry, by optimistically creating secondary indexes for primary keys and columns with uniqueness constraints. When the transaction commits, those indexes will already be created on disk.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. The schema is also shown in the image below:  Give the SQL query that returns the Name and total Revenue for Customers in the City of 'Nijmegen' in the Year 2021.
Answer: SELECT      City.Name,     SUM(F.Revenue) AS Total_Revenue FROM      Fact_Table F JOIN      Customer_DIM City ON F.Customer_ID = City.Customer_ID JOIN      Time_DIM T ON F.Time_ID = T.Time_ID WHERE      City.City = 'Nijmegen'     AND T.Year = 2021 GROUP BY      City.Name;

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for many small transactions. These transactions will all be optimistically written (assuming a ROLLBACK will not occur) to the database file, so the intermediate states do not have to be stored in temporary files.

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. The schema is also shown in the image below:  Give the SQL query that returns the Name and total Revenue for Customers in the City of 'Nijmegen' in the Year 2021.
Answer: SELECT      c.Name,      SUM(f.Revenue) AS Total_Revenue FROM      Fact_Table f JOIN      Customer_DIM c ON f.Customer_ID = c.Customer_ID JOIN      Time_DIM t ON f.Time_ID = t.Time_ID WHERE      c.City = 'Nijmegen'      AND t.Year = 2021 GROUP BY      c.Name;

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for loading large datasets, and will not be able to hold the transaction-local state in main memory. Instead of writing intermediate data to temporary files, it optimistically writes them to the database file (assuming a ROLLBACK is rare).

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for finding a single table entry, by optimistically creating secondary indexes for primary keys and columns with uniqueness constraints. When the transaction commits, those indexes will already be created on disk.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB prepares for a ROLLBACK or similar error during loading. In this case, discarding of the transaction-local state is free and optimistic.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for many small transactions. These transactions will all be optimistically written (assuming a ROLLBACK will not occur) to the database file, so the intermediate states do not have to be stored in temporary files.

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. The schema is also shown in the image below:  Give the SQL query that returns the Name and total Revenue for Customers in the City of 'Nijmegen' in the Year 2021.
Answer: SELECT Customer_DIM.Name AS CustomerName, SUM(Fact_Table.Revenue) AS TotalRevenue FROM Fact_Table JOIN Customer_DIM ON Fact_Table.Customer_ID = Customer_DIM.Customer_ID JOIN Time_DIM ON Fact_Table.Time_ID = Time_DIM.Time_ID WHERE Customer_DIM.City = 'Nijmegen' AND Time_DIM.Year = 2021 GROUP BY Customer_DIM.name;

Answer: SELECT C.Name, SUM(F.Revenue) FROM Fact_Table F JOIN Customer_DIM C ON F.Customer_ID = C.Customer_ID JOIN Time_DIM T ON F.Time_ID = T.Time_ID WHERE C.City = 'Nijmegen' AND T.Year = 2021 GROUP BY C.Name;

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for many small transactions. These transactions will all be optimistically written (assuming a ROLLBACK will not occur) to the database file, so the intermediate states do not have to be stored in temporary files.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB prepares for a ROLLBACK or similar error during loading. In this case, discarding of the transaction-local state is free and optimistic.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. The schema is also shown in the image below:  Give the SQL query that returns the Name and total Revenue for Customers in the City of 'Nijmegen' in the Year 2021.
Answer: SELECT C.Name, SUM(F.Revenue) AS Total_Revenue FROM Fact_Table F, Customer_DIM C, Time_DIM T WHERE F.Customer_ID = C.Customer_ID AND F.Time_ID = T.Time_ID AND C.City = 'Nijmegen' AND T.Year = 2021 GROUP BY C.Name;

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. Answer: SELECT C.Name, R.Revenue_total FROM ( SELECT Customer_ID, SUM(Revenue) AS Revenue_total FROM Fact_Table F, Time_DIM T WHERE F.Time_ID = T.Time_ID AND T.Year = 2021 GROUP BY Customer_ID ) R, Customer_DIM C WHERE R.Customer_ID = C.Customer_ID AND C.City = 'Nijmegen';

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. Answer: SELECT c.Name, SUM(f.Revenue) AS Total_Revenue FROM Fact_Table f JOIN Customer_DIM c ON f.Customer_ID = c.Customer_ID JOIN Time_DIM t ON f.Time_ID = t.Time_ID WHERE c.City = 'Nijmegen' AND t.Year = 2021 GROUP BY c.Name;

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. Answer: SELECT c.Name, Sum(f.revenue) FROM Fact_Table f JOIN Customer_DIM c on f.Customer_ID = c.Customer_ID JOIN Time_DIM t ON f.Time_ID = t.Time_ID WHERE c.City = 'Nijmegen' AND t.Year = 2021 GROUP BY c.Name

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB prepares for a ROLLBACK or similar error during loading. In this case, discarding of the transaction-local state is free and optimistic.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for many small transactions. These transactions will all be optimistically written (assuming a ROLLBACK will not occur) to the database file, so the intermediate states do not have to be stored in temporary files.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for loading large datasets, and will not be able to hold the transaction-local state in main memory. Instead of writing intermediate data to temporary files, it optimistically writes them to the database file (assuming a ROLLBACK is rare).

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for finding a single table entry, by optimistically creating secondary indexes for primary keys and columns with uniqueness constraints. When the transaction commits, those indexes will already be created on disk.

Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB prepares for a ROLLBACK or similar error during loading. In this case, discarding of the transaction-local state is free and optimistic.

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. The schema is also shown in the image below:  Give the SQL query that returns the Name and total Revenue for Customers in the City of 'Nijmegen' in the Year 2021.
Answer: SELECT c.Name AS Customer_Name, SUM(f.Revenue) AS Total_Revenue FROM Fact_Table f JOIN Customer_DIM c ON f.Customer_ID = c.Customer_ID JOIN Time_DIM t ON f.Time_ID = t.Time_ID WHERE c.City = 'Nijmegen' AND t.Year = 2021 GROUP BY c.Name;

When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB prepares for a ROLLBACK or similar error during loading. In this case, discarding of the transaction-local state is free and optimistic.

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. The schema is also shown in the image below:  Give the SQL query that returns the Name and total Revenue for Customers in the City of 'Nijmegen' in the Year 2021.
Answer: SELECT Name, SUM(Revenue) AS Total FROM CUSTOMER_DIM, FACT_TABLE, TIME_DIM WHERE CUSTOMER_DIM.Customer_ID = FACT_TABLE.Customer_ID   AND FACT_TABLE.Time_ID = TIME_DIM.Time_ID   AND CUSTOMER_DIM.City = 'Nijmegen'   AND TIME_DIM.Year = 2021 GROUP BY Name;

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for finding a single table entry, by optimistically creating secondary indexes for primary keys and columns with uniqueness constraints. When the transaction commits, those indexes will already be created on disk.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for many small transactions. These transactions will all be optimistically written (assuming a ROLLBACK will not occur) to the database file, so the intermediate states do not have to be stored in temporary files.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for finding a single table entry, by optimistically creating secondary indexes for primary keys and columns with uniqueness constraints. When the transaction commits, those indexes will already be created on disk.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for many small transactions. These transactions will all be optimistically written (assuming a ROLLBACK will not occur) to the database file, so the intermediate states do not have to be stored in temporary files.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for loading large datasets, and will not be able to hold the transaction-local state in main memory. Instead of writing intermediate data to temporary files, it optimistically writes them to the database file (assuming a ROLLBACK is rare).

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. The schema is also shown in the image below:  Give the SQL query that returns the Name and total Revenue for Customers in the City of 'Nijmegen' in the Year 2021.
Answer: SELECT c.Name AS Customer_Name, SUM(f.Revenue) AS Total_Revenue FROM Fact_Table f JOIN Customer_DIM c ON f.Customer_ID = c.Customer_ID JOIN Time_DIM t ON f.Time_ID = t.Time_ID WHERE c.City = 'Nijmegen' AND t.Year = 2021 GROUP BY c.Name;

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for many small transactions. These transactions will all be optimistically written (assuming a ROLLBACK will not occur) to the database file, so the intermediate states do not have to be stored in temporary files.

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB prepares for a ROLLBACK or similar error during loading. In this case, discarding of the transaction-local state is free and optimistic.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for many small transactions. These transactions will all be optimistically written (assuming a ROLLBACK will not occur) to the database file, so the intermediate states do not have to be stored in temporary files.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. The schema is also shown in the image below:  Give the SQL query that returns the Name and total Revenue for Customers in the City of 'Nijmegen' in the Year 2021.
Answer: SELECT Customer_DIM.Name, SUM(Fact_Table.Revenue) FROM Fact_Table JOIN Product_DIM ON Fact_Table.Product_ID = Product_DIM.Product_ID JOIN Time_DIM ON Fact_Table.Time_ID = Time_DIM.Time_ID JOIN Customer_DIM ON Fact_Table.Customer_ID = Customer_DIM.Customer_ID WHERE Customer_DIM.City = 'Nijmegen' AND Time_DIM.Year = 2021 GROUP BY Customer_DIM.Name;

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for many small transactions. These transactions will all be optimistically written (assuming a ROLLBACK will not occur) to the database file, so the intermediate states do not have to be stored in temporary files.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. Answer: DuckDB optimizes for finding a single table entry, by optimistically creating secondary indexes for primary keys and columns with uniqueness constraints. When the transaction commits, those indexes will already be created on disk.

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. The schema is also shown in the image below:  Give the SQL query that returns the Name and total Revenue for Customers in the City of 'Nijmegen' in the Year 2021.
Answer: SELECT c.Name, SUM(f.Revenue) AS Total_Revenue FROM Fact_Table f JOIN Time_DIM t ON f.Time_ID = t.Time_ID JOIN Customer_DIM c ON f.Customer_ID = c.Customer_ID WHERE c.City = 'Nijmegen'   AND t.Year = 2021 GROUP BY c.Name;

Answer: SELECT      Customer_DIM.Name,      SUM(Fact_Table.Revenue) AS Total_Revenue FROM      Fact_Table JOIN      Customer_DIM      ON Fact_Table.Customer_ID = Customer_DIM.Customer_ID JOIN      Time_DIM      ON Fact_Table.Time_ID = Time_DIM.Time_ID WHERE      Customer_DIM.City = 'Nijmegen'      AND Time_DIM.Year = 2021 GROUP BY      Customer_DIM.Name;

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB prepares for a ROLLBACK or similar error during loading. In this case, discarding of the transaction-local state is free and optimistic.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for finding a single table entry, by optimistically creating secondary indexes for primary keys and columns with uniqueness constraints. When the transaction commits, those indexes will already be created on disk.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for many small transactions. These transactions will all be optimistically written (assuming a ROLLBACK will not occur) to the database file, so the intermediate states do not have to be stored in temporary files.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for loading large datasets, and will not be able to hold the transaction-local state in main memory. Instead of writing intermediate data to temporary files, it optimistically writes them to the database file (assuming a ROLLBACK is rare).

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined.
